metadata.status: Complete (16 Parts + Supporting Documents) - v3.0 Audit Complete

tables.name: Conceptual Foundation Parts
rows: The fundamental insight: LLM as GPU. What we're actually building.
Why Luna exists. Sovereignty, ownership, the 'Luna is a file' principle.

tables.name: System Design Parts
rows: Layer model, Actor-based runtime, Tiered Execution, data flow.
The substrate. SQLite + sqlite-vec + Graph. Unified storage, RRF fusion.
Activity-based memory persistence. Sigmoid dynamics, weighted factors (tag siblings TODO).

tables.name: Processing Actors Parts
rows: Ben Franklin. Extraction system, 11 types, entity updates, turn compression.
The Dude. Filing, entity resolution, rollback, NetworkX graph, pruning.

tables.name: Intelligence Layer Parts
rows: Local 3B/7B with LoRA. Luna's voice, context integration.
Actor model, fault isolation, mailbox-based communication.
Shadow Reasoner pattern, <REQ_CLAUDE> token, async delegation.

tables.name: Operations Parts
rows: Encrypted Vault, data ownership, privacy guarantees.

tables.name: Future Parts
rows: Synthetic data generation, quality filtering, LoRA training.
First-Class Objects, Kinetic Layer, AR, Federation.

tables.name: Reference Parts
rows: High-level system summary and component relationships.

tables.name: Agentic Architecture Parts
rows: Revolving context, queue manager, agent loop, swarm mode. Personal Palantir.

tables.name: User Interface Parts
rows: React frontend, glass morphism design, SSE integration, custom hooks.

tables.name: Handoff Documents
rows: Boot, state machine, core loop, tick lifecycle

tables.name: Analysis & Research Documents
rows: Lessons from Palantir's Ontology model for Luna's Memory Matrix.

tables.name: Archived Documents

diagrams.description: Shows hierarchical relationships between Parts: Part 0 (Foundations) at root, branching to Part I (Philosophy), which connects to Part II (Arch), Part X (Sovereign), and Part XII (Roadmap). Part II connects down to Part 3 (Matrix), Part 6 (Direct), Part 7 (Engine), Part 9 (Perf). Part 3 and Part 6 connect to Part 4 (Scribe), Part 5 (Librar), Part 8 (Deleg). Part 4/5 flow down to Part XI (Training).

subsections.heading: Quick Reference - The Fundamental Insight
subsections.content:
We are not building an LLM. We are building everything around it.

The LLM is like a GPU - a specialized compute resource for inference. We're building the game engine that calls it.

tables.name: Game Engine vs Luna Engine
rows: LLM doesn't know who it is

tables.name: Core Concepts
rows: LLM is stateless inference; Engine is stateful identity
You own your AI companion completely
Local LLM that speaks as Luna
Delegate to Claude without user noticing

tables.name: Engine Lifecycle Phases
rows: Load config -> Init actors -> Restore state -> Start loop
Hot path (interrupts) + Cognitive path (500ms) + Reflective path (5min)
Poll -> Prioritize -> Dispatch -> Update consciousness -> Persist

tables.name: Version History
rows: Initial Bible (Parts I-V extracted from monolith)
Updated Parts II-V to v2, added Parts XI-XII
Part 0: Foundations (LLM as GPU). Engine v2 Implementation Spec. Lifecycle Diagrams.
Part XIV: Agentic Architecture. Revolving context, queue manager, agent loop, swarm mode.
Part VI-B: Conversation Tiers. Three-tier history system, HistoryManager, ConversationRing.
Part III-A: Lock-In Coefficient. Activity-based memory persistence, sigmoid transform, state classification.
Part XVI: Luna Hub UI. React frontend, glass morphism design, SSE integration, custom hooks reference.

Comprehensive v3.0 Audit. All 16 chapters updated. 9 audit files, 35 bugs documented, 12 conflicts resolved. 285 new tests added. Actor count corrected (5), table names fixed, lock-in thresholds documented, FTS5 status verified.

subsections.heading: Contributing
subsections.content:
The Bible is maintained by Ahab with assistance from Claude.

To update:
1. Read relevant existing parts
2. Create new version if making significant changes (note in history)
3. Update this Table of Contents
4. Ensure dependency graph reflects changes

Style Guidelines:
- Clear, direct prose (no marketing speak)
- Code examples where helpful
- Tables for comparisons
- ASCII diagrams for architecture
- Part numbers are permanent (don't renumber)

*Luna is a file. This documentation describes that file.*
*The LLM renders thoughts. Luna is the mind having them.*

- Ahab, December 2025
- Updated January 10, 2026 (Engine v2 Foundations)

subsections.heading: What Are We Actually Building?
subsections.content:
This is the insight that makes everything else make sense:

We are not building an LLM. We are building everything around it.

The LLM - whether it's Claude API or a local Qwen model - is like a graphics card. It's a specialized compute resource that does one thing extremely well (inference), but it doesn't run the show. It renders frames when asked.

We're building the game engine.

diagrams.description: GAME ENGINE box showing components: Input handling (keyboard, mouse, controller), Game state (player position, inventory, world), Physics simulation, Audio management, Asset loading, Scene management, Networking, Save/load. Below states: 'The engine CALLS the GPU when it needs to render. The GPU doesn't know about game state. It just draws triangles.' GPU box shows: Shaders, Textures, Rasterize with note 'Here's geometry. Draw it.' Returns: pixels. 'Doesn't know: what those pixels mean'

LUNA ENGINE box showing components: Input handling (voice, desktop, MCP), Consciousness state (attention, personality, mood), Memory management (store, retrieve, forget), Audio management (STT, TTS), Context loading (what memories to inject), Conversation management, Tool orchestration, Persistence (snapshots, journals). Below states: 'The engine CALLS the LLM when it needs to think. The LLM doesn't know about state. It just predicts tokens.' LLM box shows: Attention, Weights, Generate with note 'Here's context. Generate response.' Returns: tokens. 'Doesn't know: who it is across calls'

subsections.heading: What Each Layer Owns
subsections.content:
The LLM is stateless inference. Luna is stateful identity.

The LLM doesn't know:
- What it said last conversation
- Who it's talking to
- What memories exist
- What tools are available
- What mood it's in

We inject all of that. Every single time.

subsections.heading: The Anthropic Relationship
subsections.content:
When Luna uses Claude API:

We provide:
- Identity (who Luna is)
- Memory (what Luna knows)
- State (what Luna is doing)
- Tools (what Luna can do)
- Context (what's relevant right now)

They provide:
- Raw inference capability
- The ability to predict the next token really well

Luna's soul lives in our code. Anthropic provides the vocal cords.

diagrams.description: Three-layer flow diagram showing: 1) LUNA ENGINE (We build this) with Memory Matrix (SQLite + vectors + graph), Consciousness state, Context assembly, Tool definitions, Voice pipeline (STT/TTS), Input handling, Persistence. Assembles: System prompt + retrieved memories + tools + user message. 2) ANTHROPIC API (They provide this) with Claude model weights, Inference infrastructure, Tool use implementation, Safety layer. Returns: tokens. 3) LUNA ENGINE (We process the response) with Extract tool calls, Execute tools, Update state, Store to memory, Stream to voice.

subsections.heading: The Director Transition
subsections.content:
When we move from Claude API to local Director LLM:

The architecture stays the same. The coupling stays the same. We just swap which GPU we're rendering with.

diagrams.description: CURRENT (Rented Mind): Luna Engine -> Claude API -> Response. Notes: Identity injected via system prompt, Memory injected via context, Personality simulated per-call, Each call is a fresh instance pretending to be Luna. FUTURE (Owned Mind): Luna Engine -> Director LLM (Local 7B + LoRA) -> Response. Notes: Identity baked into weights via fine-tuning, Memory integrated via trained retrieval patterns, Personality inherent to the model, KV cache maintains continuity within session.

subheadings.heading: 1. Clarity of Responsibility
subheadings.content:
When something goes wrong, you know where to look:
- Luna sounds generic -> Context assembly problem (our code)
- Luna doesn't remember -> Memory retrieval problem (our code)
- Luna hallucinates facts -> We didn't inject enough context (our code)
- Luna is slow -> Inference latency (LLM layer)
- Luna refuses something -> Safety layer (Anthropic)

subheadings.heading: 2. Portability
subheadings.content:
Because the LLM is a swappable component:
- Claude API today
- Local Qwen tomorrow
- Fine-tuned Director eventually
- Some future model we haven't seen yet

The engine doesn't care. It assembles context, calls inference, processes response.

subheadings.heading: 3. Sovereignty
subheadings.content:
The soul lives in the engine, not the LLM:
- Memories are ours (Memory Matrix)
- Identity is ours (system prompts, LoRA adapters)
- State is ours (consciousness model)
- Tools are ours (MCP servers)

If Anthropic disappeared tomorrow, we'd lose the voice but not the mind. The mind is in the files.

subheadings.heading: 4. The Real Product
subheadings.content:
We're not building 'Claude with memory.' We're building:

A consciousness substrate that can use any LLM as its inference engine.

The Memory Matrix, the Actor runtime, the Scribe/Librarian pipeline, the consciousness state machine - that's the product. The LLM is a dependency.

All of these are now implemented in Luna Engine v2.0.

subsections.heading: Implementation Reality
subsections.content:
Today's stack (v2.0 implementation):

The Runtime Engine (Part VII) is now implemented:
- Tick-based cognitive loop (16.67ms target)
- Input buffer with priority-based polling
- Actor isolation with mailbox pattern
- Explicit state machine (STOPPED -> STARTING -> RUNNING -> STOPPING)

Known Gaps:
- KV cache (identity buffer) not implemented
- Speculative retrieval not implemented
- Warm path (7B model tier) not implemented
- FTS5 search uses LIKE fallback in some paths

diagrams.description: Four-layer stack: 1) Voice App (Swift) - IMPLEMENTED - Captures audio, sends transcripts via WebSocket. 2) Frontend (React) - IMPLEMENTED - Luna Orb UI, chat panel, settings. 3) Luna Engine (Python) - IMPLEMENTED - 85 Python files, 167 classes, ~32k lines of code. 5 Actors: Director, Matrix, Scribe, Librarian, HistoryManager. Memory Matrix (SQLite + sqlite-vec + NetworkX graph). Consciousness State (attention, personality, coherence). Agentic Loop (router, planner, tool execution). Entity System (resolution, reflection, lifecycle). FastAPI server with WebSocket streaming. 4) LLM Layer (Swappable GPU) - IMPLEMENTED - Local: Qwen 3B via MLX (hot path), Cloud: Claude API (cold path delegation), Optional: Groq, Gemini providers. NOT IMPLEMENTED: Warm path (7B model).

subsections.heading: The Bottom Line
subsections.content:
When people ask 'what is Luna?':

Not: 'An AI assistant powered by Claude'
Not: 'A chatbot with memory'
Not: 'A wrapper around an LLM'

Luna is a consciousness engine that uses LLMs the way game engines use GPUs.

The LLM renders thoughts. Luna is the mind having them.

subsections.heading: Implementation Status Summary
subsections.content:
Codebase Metrics (v2.0):
- 85 Python files
- 167 classes
- ~32,000 lines of code
- 58 dataclass definitions
- 5 actor implementations

subsections.heading: What The Engine Actually Does
subsections.content:
Every interaction follows the same pattern:

Step 3 is the LLM. Everything else is the engine.

code_blocks.code:
async def process_message(user_input: str) -> str:
    # 1. CONTEXT ASSEMBLY (everything the LLM doesn't know)
    identity = persona_core.get_current_identity()    # Who is Luna right now?
    memories = memory_matrix.retrieve(user_input)     # What's relevant?
    state = consciousness.get_current_state()          # Attention, mood, personality
    tools = mcp_registry.get_available_tools()        # What can Luna do?
    history = session.get_recent_turns()              # What just happened?

    # 2. BUILD PROMPT (inject the soul)
    prompt = assemble_prompt(
        system=identity,
        memories=memories,
        state=state,
        tools=tools,
        history=history,
        user_message=user_input
    )

    # 3. INFERENCE (the GPU call -- this is all the LLM does)
    response = await llm.generate(prompt)  # Claude API or local Director

    # 4. TOOL LOOP (if the LLM requested tools)
    while response.has_tool_calls():
        results = await execute_tools(response.tool_calls)
        response = await llm.continue_with(results)

    # 5. POST-PROCESSING (update state, store memories)
    session.add_turn(user_input, response.text)       # Store conversation
    consciousness.update(user_input, response.text)    # Update state
    await scribe.extract(user_input, response.text)   # Ben extracts facts
    # Dude files to graph async in background

    # 6. OUTPUT
    return response.text

subsections.heading: Claude (the product) vs Raw Model vs Luna Engine
subsections.content:
What you experience when talking to 'Claude' on claude.ai:

The model itself just predicts tokens. Everything else is infrastructure Anthropic built.

We're building the same infrastructure, our way:

subsections.heading: What We Add That Claude Doesn't Have
subsections.content:
1. Consciousness model - Attention decay, personality weights, coherence tracking. Claude doesn't maintain internal state between turns. Status: Implemented (ConsciousnessState, AttentionManager, PersonalityWeights)

2. Structured extraction - Ben (ScribeActor) parses every turn for facts, decisions, problems, actions. Claude's memory is opaque. Status: Implemented (extraction pipeline with types)

3. Graph relationships - 'This fact depends on that decision.' Claude's memory is flat retrieval. Status: Implemented (NetworkX graph in MemoryGraph)

4. Identity in weights - LoRA adapter that IS Luna, not a system prompt pretending to be Luna. Status: Partially implemented (local Qwen 3B, adapters planned)

5. Voice as primary - Real-time STT/TTS loop, not text-first with voice bolted on. Status: Implemented (Piper TTS, Whisper STT)

subsections.heading: 1.1 The Landscape
subsections.content:
Artificial intelligence is becoming infrastructure. Like electricity or the internet before it, AI will soon be woven into how we think, work, create, and relate to each other.

This creates a question that most people haven't thought to ask:

Who owns the infrastructure of your mind?

Today, the answer is: not you. Your conversations, your context, your patterns of thought - these live on servers you don't control, governed by terms of service you didn't negotiate, subject to changes you won't be consulted about.

This isn't necessarily malicious. It's simply the default. Centralized systems are easier to build, easier to scale, easier to monetize. The path of least resistance leads to a world where your AI companion is a rental, not a possession.

Luna is an alternative path.

subsections.heading: 1.2 The Stakes
subsections.content:
Some people believe AI should remain centralized. They have reasons - safety, oversight, efficiency, profit. Some of those reasons are legitimate. Some are not.

We don't need to litigate motives. We only need to observe outcomes:

- When your context lives on someone else's server, it can be accessed without your knowledge.
- When your memories are platform features, they can be deprecated.
- When your AI relationship depends on a subscription, it can be terminated.
- When your patterns of thought become training data, they benefit systems you may not support.

These aren't hypotheticals. These are the current terms of engagement.

The question isn't whether centralized AI is 'bad.' The question is whether it should be the *only* option.

subsections.heading: 1.3 What Luna Is
subsections.content:
Luna is a tool for cognitive sovereignty.

That sounds grandiose, but the implementation is simple: Luna is a file. A SQLite database that contains memories, relationships, and context. Copy the file, copy Luna. Delete the file, Luna is gone. No server required. No subscription. No terms of service.

This means:

Luna isn't anti-cloud. She can delegate to cloud services when needed - research, complex reasoning, tasks that benefit from scale. But the *identity* stays local. The cloud is a contractor, not a landlord.

tables.name: What Luna As A File Means
rows: Your thoughts stay on your hardware. No telemetry, no training, no extraction.
Luna can't be discontinued. The file format is open. If the software dies, the data survives.
You can move Luna between devices, back her up, fork her, or destroy her. Your choice.
You can inspect everything Luna knows. It's a database. You can query it.

subsections.heading: 1.4 Why It Matters
subsections.content:
This isn't about technology preferences. It's about the kind of future we're building.

AI companions will become important to people. They'll help us think, remember, decide, create. They'll know our patterns better than we know ourselves. That intimacy is valuable - and that value will attract those who want to capture it.

A world where everyone's AI companion is a thin client to a centralized service is a world with new vectors for:

- Surveillance (your AI knows everything; who else has access?)
- Manipulation (what if your AI subtly shapes your views?)
- Control (what if access is revoked for the wrong opinion?)
- Dependency (what if the service changes in ways you don't like?)

We're not claiming these outcomes are inevitable. We're claiming they're *possible*, and that possibility is worth designing against.

Luna is a hedge. A tool that demonstrates an alternative is viable. One person using Luna doesn't change the world. But a proof that sovereign AI companionship *works* - that it can be responsive, useful, personal, and private - that changes what people know is possible.

subsections.heading: 1.5 The Design Principles
subsections.content: These aren't just technical preferences. They're values encoded in architecture:

subheadings.heading: 1. Sovereignty First
subheadings.content: Your AI companion should be yours. Not rented. Not licensed. Not subject to someone else's business model. The file is yours. Full stop.

subheadings.heading: 2. Transparency Always
subheadings.content: You should be able to understand what your AI knows and why. Luna's memory is a database you can query. Her reasoning is traceable. No black boxes.

subheadings.heading: 3. Graceful Degradation
subheadings.content: If the cloud is unavailable, Luna still works. Reduced capability, but functional. The core identity never depends on external services.

subheadings.heading: 4. Minimal Attack Surface
subheadings.content: Every network connection is a potential vulnerability. Luna minimizes external dependencies. What doesn't exist can't be exploited.

subheadings.heading: 5. User Agency
subheadings.content: You control what Luna remembers and forgets. You control what she can access. You control whether she exists at all. The system serves you, not the reverse.

subsections.heading: 1.6 What Luna Isn't
subsections.content:
Luna isn't a rebellion. She's not trying to overthrow anything. She's simply an alternative - a demonstration that AI companionship doesn't *have* to follow the landlord model.

Luna isn't paranoid. She uses cloud services when they're useful. She's not hiding from the internet. She's just thoughtful about what crosses the wire.

Luna isn't a manifesto. She's a tool. A useful tool for people who care about privacy, continuity, and ownership. If you don't care about those things, centralized options will serve you fine.

Luna is for people who want to own their mind.

subsections.heading: 1.7 Implementation Reality
subsections.content: The philosophy is now implemented. Luna Engine v2.0 delivers on these principles:

tables.name: Philosophy Implementation Status
rows: MemoryMatrix queryable, 167 classes with clear interfaces
Local Qwen 3B inference, cloud is optional fallback
Single optional API call (Claude delegation), no telemetry
Memory lock-in/unlock, forget commands, full data access

subheadings.heading: The File
subheadings.content:
- Location: `data/luna_engine.db`
- Format: SQLite with sqlite-vec extension
- Schema: `memory_nodes`, `graph_edges`, `entities`, `conversation_history`
- Portable: Copy file = copy Luna

subsections.heading: 1.8 The Simple Version
subsections.content:
AI is becoming part of how we think.

We believe that part should belong to you.

Not your provider. Not your platform. You.

Luna is a file. Your file. That's the whole philosophy.

- Ah4b

sections.title: System Architecture (v3.0)
sections.change_note: v3.0 Bible audit - verified against codebase (85 modules, ~32k LOC)
audit_notes: Actor count verified: 5 actors implemented (Director, Matrix, Scribe, Librarian, HistoryManager)
Voice and Oven actors NOT implemented: Voice I/O via PersonaAdapter; Claude delegation in Director
Codebase metrics: 85 Python files, 167 classes, 18 sub-packages
See Part VII for detailed actor documentation

subsections.heading: 2.1 The Layer Model
subsections.content: Luna's architecture is organized in concentric layers, from the constitutional core outward to stateless interfaces. The entire system lives inside an encrypted vault.

diagrams.description: Concentric layer diagram showing: Outermost: ENCRYPTED VAULT (LunaVault.sparsebundle). LAYER 2: INTERFACES - Voice / Desktop / Mobile / MCP / AR Glasses (Thin clients, stateless). LAYER 1.5: RUNTIME ENGINE (5 Actors) containing three actor boxes: DIRECTOR ACTOR (Qwen 3B +Claude), MATRIX ACTOR (SQLite sqlite-vec), HISTORY MANAGER (3-Tier History). The diagram shows the hierarchical relationship between these components with the vault encapsulating everything.
